chunk_summary: This section outlines the milk bank processing workflow from donor intake to distribution, emphasizing quarantine-and-release principles, pasteurization, microbiology testing, and traceability to ensure safe donor human milk (DHM) handling.
learning_objectives: Understand the step-by-step workflow of milk bank processing from donor intake to distribution.
Learn the critical quality control measures, including pasteurization and microbiology testing.
Recognize the importance of traceability in donor human milk (DHM) distribution.
Identify stop rules and contingency protocols for ensuring milk safety.
key_facts: Donor milk is pasteurized at 62.5°C for 30 minutes, followed by rapid cooling to 4°C.
Post-pasteurization microbiology testing is mandatory, with significant growth leading to batch discard.
Milk is stored at -20°C with backup power contingency to maintain cold-chain integrity.
Each batch is assigned a unique identifier linking donor codes to ensure traceability.
Distribution of DHM is strictly against clinical requests, with bottle codes recorded for recipient tracking.
reference_quote: Donor milk is pasteurized at 62.5°C for 30 minutes with a documented time–temperature trace and calibrated probe, then rapidly cooled to 4°C. (Source: https://media.path.org/documents/Human_Milk_Bank_eCLMC_final.pdf)
